summary: This post catalogs the enormous depth of technical knowledge gaps exposed by the deceptively simple question 'what happens when you load a URL?' Through 48 progressively deeper questions spanning keyboards, networks, fiber optics, browsers, and displays, Luu demonstrates how much complexity lies beneath everyday computing tasks. Rather than providing answers, the post serves as a humbling inventory of how little even experienced engineers truly understand about the full stack. The post concludes with curated resources for learning about these topics and a note that this open-ended exploration represents the opposite of effective technical interviews.
key_points: The question 'what happens when you load a URL' reveals enormous technical knowledge gaps across the entire computing stack
Even experts lack deep understanding of most layers - from keyboard hardware to fiber optic transmission to browser rendering
The complexity spans physical engineering (connectors, fiber), protocols (TCP, DNS, HTTP), and systems (OS, browser, GPU)
Open-ended questions like this make poor interview filters compared to more focused technical assessments
The post provides curated resources for learning about different layers of the stack
money_quotes: I've been hearing this question a lot lately, and when I do, it reminds me how much I don't know.

You have this connection that can handle 10Gb/s. That surely won't work if there's any gap at all between the physical doodads that are being connected. How do people design connectors that can withstand tens of thousands of insertions and still maintain their tolerances?
Where I can get answers to this stuff? That's not a rhetorical question!

As an interview question, this is pretty much the antithesis of the tptacek strategy. From what I've seen, my guess is that tptacek-style interviews are much better filters than open ended questions like this.
key_insight: The staggering complexity hidden beneath simple computing tasks reveals how specialized modern engineering has become - no single person can deeply understand the full stack from keyboard physics to fiber optics to browser rendering.
